title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
empirical_scope_or_sample: Two online survey experiments with nationally representative U.S. adults; climate-policy Survey B fielded Nov–Dec 2024 with roughly 5,000–6,000 respondents after quality screens.
exact_tax_terms_used: carbon tax with cash transfers; progressive carbon tax
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Survey B describes a hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers that raises gasoline prices by $0.40/gal and rebates revenues as $600/year per adult, plus a progressive carbon tax on high-income households, and studies how emotional treatments and covariates affect support.
determinants: Anger treatment branch (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_main_result] ; Fear treatment branch (n.a.) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Positive emotion treatment branch (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Trump (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=political_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Gender: Female (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=demographic_control; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Age: 50-69 years old (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=demographic_control; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Anger treatment branch (+) [progressive_carbon_tax; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_main_result] ; Fear treatment branch (n.a.) [progressive_carbon_tax; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Trump (-) [progressive_carbon_tax; role=political_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Gender: Female (-) [progressive_carbon_tax; role=demographic_control; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Age: 30-49 years old (-) [progressive_carbon_tax; role=demographic_control; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Age: 50-69 years old (-) [progressive_carbon_tax; role=demographic_control; main=yes_secondary_result]

title: Globally representative evidence on the actual and perceived support for climate action
authors: Peter Andre; Teodora Boneva; Felix Chopra; Armin Falk
geographic_scope: Global (125 countries); carbon-tax validation evidence from United States only
empirical_scope_or_sample: 129,902 individuals aged 15+ from 125 countries in Gallup World Poll 2021/2022; separate US online survey of about 2,000 respondents used for validation analyses including carbon-tax support.
exact_tax_terms_used: a carbon tax on fossil fuels
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: The main globally representative survey measures general climate-action preferences. A separate representative US online survey shows that general demand for more climate action correlates with support for a carbon tax on fossil fuels.
determinants: demand for more political action (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result]

title: Carbon pricing in climate policy: seven reasons, complementary instruments, and political economy considerations
authors: Andrea Baranzini; Jeroen C. J. M. van den Bergh; Stefano Carattini; Richard B. Howarth; Emilio Padilla; Jordi Roca
geographic_scope: Global review, with specific examples from Switzerland, British Columbia, and other countries
empirical_scope_or_sample: Narrative review of theoretical and empirical work on carbon pricing, including studies of carbon and energy taxes and their public acceptability.
exact_tax_terms_used: carbon taxes; carbon tax; energy tax; progressive energy tax; carbon tax revenues
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Narrative review of carbon pricing that highlights carbon and energy taxes, examining their distributional impacts, lobbying, revenue recycling options, and how these design features and political-economy factors influence public and voter acceptability.
determinants: designs that protect low-income households (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=policy_design_feature; main=yes_main_result] ; Concern about income inequality [in Swiss 2015 energy tax ballot] (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; progressive energy tax (+) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=policy_design_feature; main=yes_main_result] ; information on its distributional properties are made salient (+) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_main_result] ; earmarking carbon tax revenues ... for environmental projects or other issues (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; importance of environmental organizations in successfully spreading support for British Columbia’s carbon tax (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result]

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
empirical_scope_or_sample: Systematic PRISMA review of 48 empirical studies (surveys, experiments, qualitative work) on public attitudes to carbon and fuel taxation, 2004–March 2023.
exact_tax_terms_used: carbon tax; carbon taxation; fuel taxes; gasoline taxation; fuel taxation; environmental tax reform
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Synthesizes how different revenue recycling designs and related factors (fairness, effectiveness, trust, information, experience) shape public acceptability, support and willingness to pay for carbon and fuel taxes across 48 empirical studies.
determinants: environmental earmarking (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Subsidies to low-carbon technologies and energy efficiency (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Environmental infrastructure (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Climate change adaptation (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Funding research and technical innovation (mixed) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Social cushioning (mixed) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Lump-sum transfers (i.e. fee-and-dividend policies) (mixed) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; main=yes_main_result] ; Reducing corporate taxes (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; reducing public debt / deficit reduction (mixed) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_secondary_result] ; supplementing the government budget / general budget (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; perceived fairness… with a specific emphasis on distributional fairness (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Carbon pricing policies can be regressive… (-) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; salience and communication of revenue recycling (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; The carbon tax in British Columbia became acceptable over time (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result]

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
empirical_scope_or_sample: 89 eligible datasets from 51 articles; N=119,465 participants
exact_tax_terms_used: fuel taxation; carbon taxes; fuel tax*; carbon tax*; CO2-tax; carbon dioxide tax; greenhouse gas tax
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Meta-analysis of determinants of public acceptability/support toward climate-change taxes and laws, explicitly including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
empirical_scope_or_sample: 2,826 Swiss communes; municipal-level approval rates for three 2000 federal referendums.
exact_tax_terms_used: solar initiative; energy conservation package; green tax reform; tax on fossil and nuclear energy; tax on non-renewable energy
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Uses municipal referendum returns to study support for three Swiss 2000 taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy, including one proposal with revenue recycling through lower social security contributions.
determinants: Left-green (+) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=political_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Env. behavior (+) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Young (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=demographic_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Elderly (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=demographic_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Education (+) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=socioeconomic_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Car commuter (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=socioeconomic_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Income / Income2 (mixed) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=socioeconomic_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Gender (mixed) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=demographic_control; main=yes_secondary_result] ; French (mixed) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=country_context; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Urbanity (mixed) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=country_context; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Graubünden (+) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=country_context; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Fossil fuels (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Electricity (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Paper (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result]

title: When worry about climate change leads to climate action: How values, worry and personal responsibility relate to various climate actions
authors: Thijs Bouman; Mark Verschoor; Casper J. Albers; Gisela Böhm; Stephen D. Fisher; Wouter Poortinga; Lorraine Whitmarsh; Linda Steg
empirical_scope_or_sample: European Social Survey Round 8 climate change and energy module; N=44,387 respondents
exact_tax_terms_used: increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Uses ESS Round 8 to model determinants of support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels (oil, gas, coal) across 23 countries; psychological variables include biospheric values, climate worry, and personal responsibility.
determinants: Biospheric values (mixed) [fossil_fuel_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Worry about climate change (+) [fossil_fuel_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Feelings of personal responsibility to reduce climate change (+) [fossil_fuel_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result]

title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
authors: Christian Bretter; Felix Schulz
geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
empirical_scope_or_sample: Online representative samples (quotas for age, gender, income); N=11,964 across six countries; no exclusions reported.
exact_tax_terms_used: carbon border tax
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Measures support for a “taxes” policy instrument composite including a carbon-emissions–based carbon border tax on imports, and estimates predictors of that support across six Global North/South countries.
determinants: biospheric values (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; individualistic worldviews (-) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; trust in public institutions (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; self-reported climate change knowledge (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; free-market beliefs (-) [carbon_tax; role=political_control; main=yes_main_result] ; aggregated region (Global North versus Global South) (+) [carbon_tax; role=country_context; main=yes_secondary_result] ; individualistic worldviews × aggregated region (-) [carbon_tax; role=country_context; main=yes_secondary_result] ; trust in public institutions × aggregated region (+) [carbon_tax; role=country_context; main=yes_secondary_result] ; biospheric values × aggregated region (n.a.) title: Denial and distrust: explaining the partisan climate gap
authors: Dylan Bugden
empirical_scope_or_sample: Non-probability Prolific survey of self-identified Democrats/Republicans (n=1808), collected Mar 22–30, 2021.
exact_tax_terms_used: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Respondents indicated support/oppose for an explicit carbon tax item; Kitagawa–Blinder–Oaxaca decompositions (Table 3, carbon-tax column) estimate how the Republicans–Democrats support gap is explained by trust in science, climate-science denial, and (smaller) trust in government.
determinants: Trust (science) (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Climate denial (-) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Trust (gov’t) (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result]

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
empirical_scope_or_sample: VOX survey after Swiss 2015 energy-tax initiative ballot (N≈1500); plus choice experiment (N=1200) administered March-May 2015 on representative Swiss population.
exact_tax_terms_used: energy tax; value-added tax (VAT); carbon tax (CHF/tCO2); lump-sum transfers; social cushioning; international carbon offsets
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Studies acceptability of a Swiss referendum on replacing VAT with an energy tax on non-renewable energy, and a full-information choice experiment for a carbon tax (CHF/tCO2) with alternative revenue recycling designs.
determinants: Competitiveness effects (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Regressive effects (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Environmental effectiveness (+) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Revenue neutrality (+) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Energy security (+) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Number of cars in household (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=socioeconomic_control; main=no_control_or_methodological_only] ; Concern with public intervention (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=political_control; main=no_control_or_methodological_only] ; Green affinity (+) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=political_control; main=no_control_or_methodological_only] ; Tax rate (CHF/tCO2) (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=policy_design_feature; main=yes_main_result] ; Lump-sum redistribution (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Social cushioning (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Environmental recycling (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Decreasing tax base (-) [energy_tax_explicitly_carbon_or_fuel_based; role=substantive_mechanism; main=no_unsupported_or_unclear] ; VAT reduction (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=no_unsupported_or_unclear]

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
empirical_scope_or_sample: Online survey of 4,997 citizens (~1,000 per country), randomized to hypothetical carbon-tax designs; respondents report whether they would support if implemented in all other countries.
exact_tax_terms_used: global carbon tax; carbon tax; carbon taxes; fuel tax; petrol levy; climate dividends; per-capita dividend; income taxes
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Survey experiment shows public support depends strongly on revenue recycling: worldwide mitigation funding and domestic income-tax cuts/per-capita returns generate majority support across countries; global per-capita dividends are uneven, and fairness depends on whether benefits accrue to all countries.
determinants: Funding mitigation projects worldwide received the highest support (65%), on average. (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_main_result] ; These were: lowering income taxes ... (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_main_result] ; redistributing revenues domestically to each citizen (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_main_result] ; Sharing out funds per capita globally also appealed ... but not in the United States (44%). (mixed) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_secondary_result] ; could succeed if its revenues were allocated to all countries. Transferring wealth from developed to only emerging countries might trigger opposition in the former (mixed) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_secondary_result] ; A global system of harmonized carbon taxes, in which countries retain control over the revenues (mixed) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=policy_design_feature; main=yes_secondary_result] ; South Africans preferred the revenues to be shared as a dividend, because this would do more to relieve the economic burden of a carbon tax there (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_secondary_result] ; calls to ‘axe the tax’ faded once residents received rebates on income tax (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=no_unsupported_or_unclear] ; Macron’s fuel tax is seen as unfair ... revenues are not given back to citizens ... hits low-income and rural households hardest (-) [gasoline_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=no_unsupported_or_unclear] ; Most people underestimate the benefits of lower emissions and overestimate drawbacks such as job losses (-) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=no_unsupported_or_unclear] ; Acceptance increases once the policy is enacted, so implementing a tax gradually is more successful (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=no_unsupported_or_unclear] ; people tend to like ‘progressive’ policies under which costs are borne mainly by those most able to pay (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=no_unsupported_or_unclear] ; carbon taxes do not swell government coffers, which appeals to the political right (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=no_unsupported_or_unclear] ; The left is also interested because the average tax burden is unchanged and low-income households are better off (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=no_unsupported_or_unclear]

title: The public’s views on climate policies in seven large global south countries
authors: Richard T. Carson; Jiajun Lu; Emily A. Khossravi; Gunnar Köhlin; Erik Sterner; Thomas Sterner; Dale Whittington
geographic_scope: Chile, Colombia, India, Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa, Vietnam
empirical_scope_or_sample: YouGov online survey, n=8,400 adults (about 1,200 per country) collected April–May 2023 (internet-enabled samples).
exact_tax_terms_used: carbon tax revenue; revenues from carbon taxes
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Best–worst preferences over earmarks for carbon tax revenue (health/education, solar and clean R&D, targeted vs uniform rebates, roads, and deficit reduction).
determinants: Spend on education and health (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Invest in clean R&D (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Subsidize solar panels (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Return to poor (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Return in equal shares (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Reduced government deficit (mixed) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Invest in roads (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result]

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
empirical_scope_or_sample: 40,680 survey respondents across 20 countries
exact_tax_terms_used: carbon tax with cash transfers; carbon tax with progressive transfers; tax on fossil fuels ($45/t CO2)
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Large cross-country survey experiment on acceptability of carbon taxes with cash transfers/progressive transfers and a fossil-fuel tax, plus information treatments affecting support.
determinants: Believes the policy would reduce GHG emissions (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Believes the policy would reduce air pollution (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Believes the policy would have positive econ. effects (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Believes low-income earners will lose (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Believes own household would lose (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Believes the policy is fair (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; cash transfers to the poorest households / cash transfers to constrained households (+) [progressive_carbon_tax; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; fund environmental infrastructures, subsidize low-carbon technologies, or reduce income taxes (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; equal cash transfers to all households (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; reduction in corporate income taxes (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Car-dependent respondents are less supportive of taxes on fossil fuels and carbon taxes with cash transfers (-) [other_direct_carbon_or_fuel_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Climate policies treatment (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_main_result] ; Both treatments (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_main_result] ; Climate impacts treatment (n.a.) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Believes the high-income earners will lose (mixed) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result]

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
empirical_scope_or_sample: Representative French survey of 3,002 respondents (Bilendi), Feb–Mar 2019.
exact_tax_terms_used: Tax & Dividend; carbon tax; diesel tax; tax on kerosene (aviation)
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Studies French approval of a proposed +50€/tCO2 carbon tax (“Tax & Dividend”: revenue returned lump-sum to adults) and explores acceptance/determinants for diesel and kerosene fuel taxes.
determinants: overestimation of its impact on one’s purchasing power (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; without mentioning the impact on prices (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=experimental_treatment; main=yes_secondary_result] ; penalize rural households (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; pretext to increase taxes (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; ineffective to reduce pollution (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; alternatives are insufficient or too expensive (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; investments in the energy transition (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_secondary_result] ; targeted ... constrained with respect to their consumption of petroleum products (fifth with 55% approval) (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_secondary_result] ; reduction in the VAT rate (61% approval) (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Ecologist (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=political_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Yellow Vests: supports (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=political_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Age: ≥ 65 (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=demographic_control; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Sex: Male (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=demographic_control; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Size of town (1 to 5) (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=demographic_control; main=yes_secondary_result] ; own a diesel vehicle (-) [diesel_tax; role=socioeconomic_control; main=yes_main_result] ; title: Yellow Vests, Pessimistic Beliefs, and Carbon Tax Aversion
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
empirical_scope_or_sample: Representative survey of 3,002 French households (Feb–Mar 2019) plus official Insee household surveys to compute objective incidence and net gains.
exact_tax_terms_used: carbon tax; tax & dividend; tax & targeted dividend; sectoral tax & dividend; Contribution Climat-Énergie
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Studies French support/opposition to a carbon tax whose revenues are redistributed via a tax-and-dividend (uniform and targeted) scheme in the Yellow Vests context, and shows causal effects of beliefs about personal incidence and environmental effectiveness on approval.
determinants: belief that one does not lose from it (win/lose category) (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Believing that the tax is effective (environmental effectiveness) (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Progressivity belief (benefit the poorest / not regressive) (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; WTP from subjective gain (subjective net purchasing-power change) (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; pessimistic bias about net gains (overestimate losses) (-) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result]

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
empirical_scope_or_sample: Review of 46 core Web of Science studies plus additional papers from citations; includes surveys, choice experiments, and other experiments on public support/WTP
tax_instruments_analyzed: carbon_tax; carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; co2_tax; fuel_tax; gasoline_tax; fossil_fuel_tax
exact_tax_terms_used: carbon tax; CO2 taxation; fuel tax; gasoline taxation; gasoline (petrol) taxes; fossil fuel taxes; revenue-neutral carbon tax; carbon offset
carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Synthesizes determinants of public acceptability/support for carbon/CO2 and fuel taxes, focusing on perceived effectiveness, costs, fairness, trust/norms, and design features like revenue earmarking/recycling and tax-label framing.
determinants: people approved ... subsidies ... or expansion of public transportation. To a significantly lesser degree they supported two types of CO2 taxation (-) [co2_tax; role=policy_design_feature; main=yes_main_result] ; were more likely to support a fuel tax if they believed the tax was going to reduce driving and exert a positive effect on climate change (+) [fuel_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Perceived effectiveness best predicted people’s policy choices... more effective... than a carbon tax (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; overestimate the costs of taxes and underestimate the ‘hidden’ costs of alternative policies (-) [unclear_tax_instrument; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; distribution of costs is progressive... richer members... pay a larger share and potential revenues are redistributed (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; support for fairness principles ‘equality’ and ‘need’... strongly positive impact on tax support (+) [fuel_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; WTP... decrease if general taxpayers are assigned great responsibility... increase if industry and energy users are targeted (-) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; acceptability of a carbon tax can vary between 68% and 48% depending on whether revenues are earmarked... or go directly to the general tax budget (+) [carbon_tax_with_revenue_recycling; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; Revenue recycling... reducing VAT or income tax — increased policy support (+) [gasoline_tax; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; support a rise in fossil fuel taxes if revenues are earmarked... whereas without earmarking... prefers a decrease (+) [fossil_fuel_tax; role=revenue_or_recycling_design; main=yes_main_result] ; policy support requires trust in government and politicians, as exemplified by the case of carbon taxation in Sweden and Norway (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; trust in environmental scientists and environmental organizations... trust in industry negatively associated with greater policy support (-) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_secondary_result] ; support for a carbon tax falls when people receive information that only a minority of other citizens support the proposed tax (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; a majority of participants favoured a carbon tax after deliberation (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Republicans preferred to pay more... labelled as a carbon offset than... labelled as a carbon tax (no difference for Democrats) (mixed) [carbon_tax; role=policy_design_feature; main=yes_main_result] ; improved public transport can reduce some public opposition to gasoline (petrol) taxes (+) [gasoline_tax; role=policy_design_feature; main=yes_main_result]

title: Understanding the resistance to carbon taxes: Drivers and barriers among the general public and fuel-tax protesters
authors: Jens Ewald, Thomas Sterner, Erik Sterner

determinants: Protester (ref. National sample) (-) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Education (+) [carbon_tax; role=demographic_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Income (n.a.) [carbon_tax; role=socioeconomic_control; main=yes_secondary_result] ; Residence (-) [carbon_tax; role=demographic_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Trust in government (-) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result] ; Political orientation (+) [carbon_tax; role=political_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Political orientation (-) [carbon_tax; role=political_control; main=yes_main_result] ; Pigouvian principle (+) [carbon_tax; role=substantive_mechanism; main=yes_main_result]

title: Majority support for global redistributive and climate policies
authors: Adrien Fabre; Thomas Douenne; Linus Mattauch

title: Understanding Public Acceptability of Climate Policies in Europe
authors: Shouyu Zhang; Susana Ferreira; Berna Karali
